title: Tinnai Swami
subtitle: Dec 12, 1912 - Dec 7, 2003
meta_description: Sri Tinnai Swami (Ramaswami, 1912–2003) was a jivanmukta disciple of Ramana Maharshi who, upon being told 'Iru' (Be) by Bhagavan, remained for over fifty years on a masonry bench in Tiruvannamalai in continuous Self-abidance.
body_html:
Early in the morning on Deepam Day, 7th December 2003, a little-known devotee left his physical body in Tiruvannamalai, where he had lived for more than 54 years in the supreme state of atma-jnana bestowed upon him by the Grace of Sri Bhagavan.

The devotee I am writing about was in his former life named Ramaswami, but for more than 40 years he has been known as Sri Tinnai Swami, because he lived on and seldom moved away from the tinnai (masonry bench) in the verandah of the house of the family of Sri C.P. Nathan.

Sri Tinnai Swami was born in Coimbatore on the 12th December 1912, in a family of lawyers and doctors belonging to the small Telugu Brahmin community. As a young man he was employed as a biochemist in Madras Medical College, during which time he married and had four sons. Until his mid-thirties there was no indication of the great inner transformation that was to happen later.

In the mid-1940's he came to Tiruvannamalai on three occasions to have darshan of Sri Bhagavan. Each visit caused an immediate change in his outward life. Coming for the fourth time after resigning his job on principle, he wrote to his wife planning to stay longer. During those months, his state of mind was changing profoundly. When his mother asked if he had been to see Swami, he replied: "What is the use of merely seeing Swami? We must become Swami."

After his family had left, he approached Sri Bhagavan and asked permission to leave for Pondicherry to apply for a job. Sri Bhagavan replied: 'Iru' — a Tamil word that literally means 'Be' but in context means 'Stay'. Sri Muruganar, who was present, understood the great power of that single word and the profound impact it had.

Sri Tinnai Swami obeyed both the colloquial and the literal meaning of the word 'iru'. From that moment, he never left Tiruvannamalai, and he also remained fixed firmly in the eternal state of Self-abidance. As Sri Sadhu Om once commented: "Lord Murugan bestowed jnana upon Arunagiriyar and Bhagavan bestowed jnana upon Tinnai Swami by uttering just one word, 'Iru'."

From that day, his outward behaviour changed radically. He seldom spoke; when he did, his words were usually enigmatic, often allegorical. He neglected his appearance, allowed his hair to grow long and matted, and lived by begging his food — usually just one meal a day. He was known sometimes to remain so absorbed in his inward state that for several days he did not stir outwardly even to eat.

His wife came to Tiruvannamalai on learning of his transformation and asked to serve him. He agreed for a while, then told her that her duty was to look after their sons. Over the next fifty years she visited on many occasions and served him whenever he allowed it.

Finally one day in the late 1950s, when he came to the house of Sri C.P. Nathan as usual, he said: "Amma, it is raining outside. May I take shelter here?" She replied that he was welcome to stay. He sat on the tinnai in their verandah and remained there. It was actually a hot sunny day in Purattasi — a small cloud appeared and a few drops fell. What he really meant was that living in public like Virupaksha cave, he was exposed to unwanted attention.

When C.P. Nathan needed a new house, Sri Tinnai Swami pointed to a nearby vacant land saying, "Your house is built there," and predicted that "Three sadhus will build your house for you" — and so it happened, with Sri Sadhu Om and two other sadhus providing the help.

For more than 40 years Sri Tinnai Swami lived on that tinnai. He left the compound on only a very few occasions. Towards the end of 1985, Sri Tinnai Swami lost his eyesight, but his state remained unshakable as ever. He left his body as quietly as he had lived in it, in the early hours of December 7, 2003, a few hours before the holy Deepam was lit on Arunachala. He was interred beside the samadhi shrine of Sri Sadhu Om.

As Sri Bhagavan says in verse 303 of Guru Vachaka Kovai: "The life of sat-achara (abidance in the state of being) lived by a jnani in this material world is itself that ucchistha" — the sacred leftovers that purify the world of all evil.

By Michael James
notes: Subtitle is dates. By Michael James. Long article.
